# Robot Framework Integration

`testid-gen-locators` reads your registry and writes one Python file per component, full of ready-to-use XPath constants:

```python
# tests/locators/order_list.py — Generated by testid-gen-locators
# Component: order-list

orderList_table_auftragsliste = "xpath://*[@data-testid='order-list__table--auftragsliste']"  # testid-managed
orderList_th_id               = "xpath://*[@data-testid='order-list__th--id']"  # testid-managed
orderList_th_kunde            = "xpath://*[@data-testid='order-list__th--kunde']"  # testid-managed
```

## The `# testid-managed` marker

Every generated line ends with `# testid-managed`. It's a small thing, but it lets an optional companion tool (a Python replacer) follow along when the differ reports a `renamed` ID: find the marker, update the constant name, keep the tests green.

## Customising the variable names

Since v0.4.0 the Python variable name is built from a **template** — the same placeholder syntax as the tagger's `idFormat`. The default is:

```json
"locators": {
  "variableFormat": "{component}_{element}_{key}"
}
```

With that template:

| Registry entry | Resulting constant |
|---|---|
| `component=order-list`, `element=dropdown`, `key=customer` | `orderList_dropdown_customer` |
| `component=login`, `element=input`, `key=email` | `login_input_email` |
| Hash-only testid, `component=order-list`, `element=button`, `key=submit` | `orderList_button_submit` |

Notice the last row: even when the testid itself is something opaque like `tid-a1b2c3`, the Python variable is still readable because the name is rebuilt from the registry's semantic data, not from the testid string.

### Why this matters

Short hash-based testids keep the HTML clean, but make raw camelCased variable names (`tidA1b2c3`) useless. With a template, you get the best of both — compact testids in the DOM, readable constants in the Python code.

### Template recipes

| Template | Example output | When to use |
|---|---|---|
| `{component}_{element}_{key}` (default) | `orderList_dropdown_customer` | General-purpose, readable. |
| `{element}_{key}` | `dropdown_customer` | Small project, component is obvious from the module filename. |
| `{component}_{key}` | `orderList_customer` | Element type is noise in your naming scheme. |
| `loc_{component}_{element}_{key}` | `loc_orderList_dropdown_customer` | Prefix every locator so IDE autocomplete surfaces them together. |
| `{component}_{element}_{key}_{hash}` | `orderList_dropdown_customer_a1b2c3` | Disambiguate when two elements would otherwise collide on semantics. |

### Override via CLI

```bash
testid gen-locators testids.latest.json --out-dir tests/locators \
  --variable-format '{element}_{key}'
```

## Using it in Robot Framework

Import the generated file as variables and reference the constants by name:

```robot
*** Settings ***
Library    SeleniumLibrary
Variables  ../locators/order_list.py

*** Test Cases ***
Order list is visible
    Wait Until Element Is Visible    ${orderList_table_auftragsliste}
```

No more hand-maintained locator files. No more hunting through the HTML to figure out which `<tr>` you meant.
